sections.content:
My relationship with aesthetics has changed a lot since the Interactive Media game, and AIBPO is probably where that change became most visible.

The game was about craft — 270 hours between two people, every model and shader made ourselves, way beyond what the assignment asked for. The result looked and felt the way we wanted, but I wasn't asking what it was communicating or why. I was just trying to make it as good as I could.

AIBPO asked that question directly. The aesthetic goal wasn't to make something look good — it was to make an audience feel the discomfort of a system we were critiquing. Ambiguity and staged authority became intentional design materials. The real limitation is that a live performance leaves almost nothing behind — the intent is inaccessible to anyone who wasn't in the room.

The shift is less about skill and more about what I was asking aesthetics to do: execution in one case, framing in the other. I don't think I've fully settled into one or the other, and I'm not sure I should. AIBPO is also where my design work and my vision overlapped most directly — a critique of techno-solutionism made experiential, designed to be felt rather than just understood.
